file_name: Dimsa and Paulauskaitė-Tarasevičienė - 2021 - Melanoma multi class segmentation using different u-net type architectures.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically three U-Net type architectures (U-Net, U-Net++, and MultiResUNet), to perform multi-class segmentation of melanoma skin lesions from the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental investigation comparing the performance of three U-Net type architectures (U-Net, U-Net++, and MultiResUNet) for multi-class melanoma segmentation. It details specific methods, dataset usage (ISIC), training parameters (epochs, batch size), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy, dice coefficient) in Table 1, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Melanoma multi class segmentation using different U-Net type architectures
brief_summary: This study evaluated the performance of three U-Net type architectures—U-Net, U-Net++, and MultiResUNet—for multi-class segmentation of melanoma skin lesions. The models were trained and tested on the ISIC dataset, which included 9,900 benign and 4,291 malignant images that were augmented and resized to 256x256 pixels. Each model was trained for 20 epochs with a batch size of 8, using Adam optimization and categorical cross-entropy loss. Performance was assessed using accuracy, loss, validation accuracy, validation loss, and the Dice coefficient. The results indicated that MultiResUNet achieved the highest Dice coefficient (73.69%) and competitive accuracy (91.45%), while U-Net++ achieved the highest overall accuracy (92.23%). The authors concluded that while modified U-Net architectures improve upon the classical model, multi-class segmentation of skin lesions remains a challenging task.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on multi-class segmentation of melanoma. The dataset contained 4,291 malignant images (labeled as melanoma in the abstract/intro context, though technically 'malignant' in the dataset split). The task involved segmenting malignant lesions.

The study performed multi-class segmentation including benign lesions. The dataset contained 9,900 benign images. The models were evaluated on their ability to segment benign skin lesions (referred to as 'skin moles' in results).

all_task_types.target: Pixel-wise masks for benign and malignant melanoma skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class segmentation of melanoma skin lesions using U-Net, U-Net++, and MultiResUNet architectures. The models predict pixel-wise masks distinguishing between benign and malignant classes (and background), evaluated using Dice coefficient and accuracy metrics on the ISIC dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive (International Skin Imaging Collaboration)
datasets_used.description: An open-source public access archive of skin lesion images including melanoma. Images and masks were downloaded using an ISIC archive downloader. The dataset contains benign and malignant skin lesions. Masks for malignant images were manually created for images where they were not provided (only 1084 malignant images had masks provided originally). Images were resized to 256x256 pixels. Only the largest skin spot in an image was taken for evaluation; smaller marks were ignored.

use.training: The dataset was augmented (rotation, width/height shift, flips, zoom) and used for training the U-Net, U-Net++, and MultiResUNet models. The text states '9900 benign images and 4291 malignant images were augmented and used for the training.'
use.validation: The same augmentation process was performed on training and validation sets, implying the dataset was split for validation, but specific counts for the validation split are not reported.
use.testing: Not explicitly reported as a separate held-out test set with counts; performance metrics (accuracy, dice coefficient) are reported for the models, likely on the validation set or a subset thereof, but the paper does not specify a distinct testing split count.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between architectures were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, loss, Dice coefficient) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Archive.notes: MultiResUNet achieved the highest Dice coefficient (73.69%) and competitive accuracy (91.45%). U-Net++ achieved the highest overall accuracy (92.23%). MultiResUNet performed 3.70% higher than U-Net and 2.61% higher than U-Net++ in terms of Dice coefficient. The classical U-Net model demonstrated the worst performance with 84.65% accuracy. All models managed to locate and segment skin moles, but output wrong type of mask for both malignant and benign lesions, reducing the Dice coefficient. MultiResUNet was the only architecture to detect benign skin lesions in the visual examples provided.

U-Net.structure: Encoder-decoder with down-sampling and up-sampling paths, each consisting of 4 blocks. Down-sampling uses two 3x3 unpadded convolutions followed by Leaky ReLU (α=0.3) and 2x2 max pooling (stride 2). Up-sampling uses 2x2 convolution to halve feature channels, concatenation with down-sampling features, and two 3x3 unpadded convolutions followed by Leaky ReLU. Number of feature channels doubles at each down-sampling block. Total 29 layers (20 convolutional, 4 max-pooling, 4 up-sampling, 1 output).
U-Net.output_layer: 1x1 convolution with softmax function for 2 classes

U-Net++.structure: Based on U-Net but redesigns skip connections. Feature maps from down-sampling path undergo dense convolution blocks before concatenation with up-sampling path feature maps. Deep supervision feature was not used.

MultiResUNet.structure: Incorporates 3x3 and 7x7 convolutions in parallel (7x7 substituted by sequence of 3x3 convolutions to reduce memory). Outputs concatenated. Residual connections and 1x1 convolutions added to shortcut connections. Res paths replace simple concatenation. Number of convolutional blocks in Res paths reduced from 4 to 1 as model goes deeper.
MultiResUNet.filters: W = 1.67 x U (where U is corresponding U-Net layer value: 32, 64, 128, 255, 512). Filters assigned as W/6, W/3, W/2 to three successive layers.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: rotation (0-360 degrees), width shift (0-8%), height shift (0-8%), horizontal flip, vertical flip, and zoom (0-20%)
Mask color adjustment: replacing black and white masks for malignant images with black and grey masks for multi-class segmentation
Selection of the largest skin spot in images for evaluation; smaller marks ignored
Manual creation of segmentation masks for malignant images where masks were not originally provided

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random width shift from 0 to 8% of total width
Random height shift from 0 to 8% of total height
Random zoom inside the image between 0 and 20%

conclusions: MultiResUNet achieved the highest Dice coefficient (73.69%) and competitive accuracy (91.45%), performing 3.70% higher than U-Net and 2.61% higher than U-Net++ in terms of Dice coefficient.
U-Net++ achieved the highest overall accuracy (92.23%), which was only 0.86% higher than MultiResUNet.
The classical U-Net model demonstrated the poorest performance with an accuracy of 84.65% and the lowest Dice coefficient (0.7096).

All three architectures (U-Net, U-Net++, and MultiResUNet) were able to locate and segment skin moles, but they outputted the wrong type of mask for both malignant and benign lesions, which reduced the Dice coefficient values.
MultiResUNet was the only architecture among the three tested that successfully detected benign skin lesions in the visual examples provided.
Modified U-Net architectures can slightly improve the performance results of the classical U-Net model.
Multi-class segmentation of skin lesions remains a challenging task despite the success of U-Net type architectures in single-class medical image segmentation.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation, relying solely on the ISIC dataset which may introduce population or dataset bias.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to compare the performance differences between the architectures.
Manual creation of segmentation masks was required for the majority of malignant images (only 1084 had provided masks), introducing potential annotation bias.
The evaluation protocol ignored smaller skin spots in images, potentially underestimating model performance on multi-lesion cases.
All models exhibited a tendency to output the wrong type of mask for both malignant and benign lesions, indicating a limitation in class-specific segmentation accuracy despite locating the lesions.
The MultiResUNet model showed signs of overfitting after 7 epochs, necessitating a retraining with fewer epochs (10) to achieve optimal results.
Hardware limitations constrained the batch size to 8, which may affect the stability of gradient updates and generalization.

The paper does not report a separate held-out test set with specific counts, raising concerns about data leakage or over-optimistic performance estimates if the validation set was used for final reporting.
